title: The Pig King
summary: A princess must break the enchantment of a pig-like king, learning that true beauty lies within.
full_text:
Once upon a time, in a kingdom far to the east, where the cities were built of ivory and the gardens bloomed with flowers that sang in the wind, there lived a king and queen who had been married for seven years but had no children. The queen grieved daily for the lack of an heir, and the king grew desperate, scouring the world for healers and seers. One summer afternoon, the queen fell asleep in her garden beneath a blooming rose bush, the air heavy with the scent of nectar and warm earth. Three fairies passed by and saw her sleeping form. The first fairy was moved by her beauty and granted her a wish: she would have a son who would be the most handsome and virtuous man in all the world, and no weapon could harm him.

The second fairy added that no one would ever be able to offend this child, for he would possess every virtue under the sun, and his wisdom would be as deep as the ocean. But the third fairy, who was bitter and cruel, her heart blackened by old grudges, whispered her curse: the child would be born with the skin and habits of a pig until he had taken a wife three times. The queen gave birth to a son who was, indeed, a pig in form—snout, curly tail, and all. The king and queen were horrified, but their love was stronger than their shame. They raised him as their son, dressing him in royal robes and teaching him the laws of state and the arts of diplomacy. He was a prince of nobility and kindness, though he would often wander out to the mud and wallow like a pig, nuzzling his parents and getting them dirty with an unabashed, animal love.

When the pig-prince grew to manhood, he demanded a wife. His parents despaired—who would look past the snout and the bristles to see the golden soul beneath? But the prince had seen a neighboring princess, a maiden of such grace and empathy that she could hear the sorrow in the wind. The king sent messengers with gifts of gold and silk, and despite the princess's initial revulsion, her father forced her to accept the match for the sake of a powerful alliance. On their wedding night, the princess hid a knife under her pillow, intending to kill the pig-prince while he slept, unable to endure the thought of a life bound to a beast.

But as she watched him sleep, she saw something that stopped her hand. In the soft light of the moon, the pig-skin seemed to shimmer and fade, revealing a man of incomparable beauty, his face peaceful and his spirit radiating a profound kindness. The pig-skin lay discarded on the floor, a heavy, coarse blanket of flesh. She took it and burned it in the fireplace, thinking she had freed him. When the prince awoke, he was horrified. "You have destroyed my skin!" he cried. "The spell required the skin to be shed naturally through three marriages. Now I must find another wife, for the spell can only be broken after three marriages!" He left in anger, though he gave her a ring as a token of his eternal gratitude for her compassion.

For years, the princess waited, her heart now bound to the prince. She learned the art of patience and the depth of true love, understanding that the pig-skin had been a mirror, reflecting the ugliness of those who judged by appearance. When the prince returned for the third time, he was no longer a pig, nor was he merely a man. He was a being of light and wisdom, his curse finally shattered. They were married in a ceremony that united their kingdoms in a bond of empathy and love, and they ruled with a kindness that became the hallmark of their reign, teaching their subjects that the most beautiful things in life are often hidden beneath a coarse exterior.
moral: Beauty lies within, not on the surface; patience and love break even the strongest curses.
source: Giovanni Francesco Straparola, The Facetious Nights

title: The Glass Mountain
summary: A brave youth overcomes an impossible ascent to rescue a princess from a crystal peak.
full_text:
In the heart of Italy, where the mountains rise like crystal teeth toward the sky, there stood in ancient times a Glass Mountain so clear and steep that no climber had ever reached its summit. The mountain was not made of stone or earth, but of a singular, seamless piece of translucent glass that reflected the sun with a blinding intensity, casting rainbows across the valley below. At the very top, in a castle made of pure gold that shimmered like a fallen star, there lived an enchanted princess of such beauty that poets wept upon hearing her name. She sat in a silver chamber, guarded by a mighty eagle whose wings could shatter the clouds and whose eyes could spot a field mouse from a league away. In her lap, she held three golden apples—the prize for any man brave enough and clever enough to climb the impossible height.

Many knights had attempted the ascent over the years. They came from distant lands, draped in shining armor that clattered with every step, riding magnificent horses bred for war. But the glass was smoother than ice, and every rider slipped, their armor sliding uselessly against the polished surface. They fell to their deaths on the jagged rocks below, and over decades, their bleached bones whitened the lower slopes, serving as a grim, silent warning to all who would try.

One day, a young schoolboy—mere twelve years old, small in stature but towering in spirit—heard the tale from his grandmother and decided he would try. His parents forbade him, fearing for his life, but the boy was driven by a curiosity and a longing he could not name. He spent weeks in the deep forest, where he eventually tracked and killed a lynx, not out of cruelty, but out of necessity. He carefully removed the animal's sharp, curved claws and fashioned them into makeshift climbing irons, attaching them to his hands and feet with strips of tough leather.

With these tools, he began his ascent at dawn, when the glass was coolest. The first day, he climbed one-third of the way up, his fingers bleeding and his muscles screaming, before descending to a narrow ledge to rest. The second day, he pushed higher, and as he reached the midpoint, the great eagle saw him. The bird swooped down with talons bared, thinking the boy was carrion come to feed on the mountain's ghosts. But the boy was no corpse. With a burst of adrenaline, he grabbed the eagle's legs and held on with all his might. The great bird, confused and enraged, flew higher than it had ever flown, unintentionally carrying the boy right to the summit.

There, in the golden castle, the eagle dropped him. In the struggle, the boy had managed to sever the bird's talons with his small knife. He fell, not onto the glass, but into a lush garden of silver trees, landing softly in the branches of a golden apple tree. The magic of the apples healed his wounds instantly. The princess, who had waited in solitude for an eternity, ran to greet him. 'You are my husband and master!' she cried, embracing the brave boy. She gave him all the treasures of the castle, but the boy valued only the princess's hand in marriage, proving that courage and cunning are the only true keys to the highest peaks.
moral: Those who work for love surpass those who buy it; cleverness and courage conquer all.
source: Italian Folklore (variant of The Princess on the Glass Hill)

title: Basilisk: The King of Serpents
summary: A rooster-born king of serpents with a deadly gaze is defeated by his own reflection, proving that the greatest power can be its own undoing.
full_text:
In the ancient city of Rome, there once lived a creature so terrible that its mere glance could kill. The basilisk, known throughout medieval Europe as the king of serpents, was said to be born from a rooster's egg hatched by a toad in the foulest of places. The Roman poet Pliny the Elder described it as a small serpent, no more than twelve inches long, with a white crown-like marking on its head that gave it its name, basiliskos, meaning "little king."

According to the old bestiaries, the basilisk possessed the most fearsome powers imaginable. Its gaze could kill instantly, turning men to stone or striking them dead on the spot. Its breath was so toxic that it withered every plant it touched, cracked stones, and poisoned the air for miles around. Wherever the basilisk crawled, the grass died and the earth became barren. So deadly was its presence that even a man on horseback who struck one with a spear would feel the poison travel up the weapon, killing both rider and horse. The creature was said to inhabit the deepest deserts of Libya, and according to Roman legend, the Sahara itself was once a fertile land until a plague of basilisks turned it into the endless waste it is today. Its hiss made all other serpents flee in terror, for even they could not withstand the king's presence.

But the basilisk had one enemy: the weasel. According to the same ancient sources, a weasel could kill a basilisk through its mere odor. Brave hunters would send weasels into basilisk dens to fight the deadly serpent. The weasel would inevitably die as well, but the basilisk would perish alongside its tiny foe. This was seen as proof that nature always provides an antidote to even the most terrible poison. Other legends claimed that the crow of a rooster could drive away or kill the basilisk, a fitting irony given its unnatural birth.

The most daring heroes, however, used a different strategy. They carried polished shields of silver and mirrors of pure glass. When the basilisk emerged from its hole, eyes blazing with lethal intent, the heroes would hold the mirrors aloft. The creature, seeing its own reflection for the first time, believed it was facing another king. It unleashed its full, deadly gaze upon the mirror, and the poison returned in a single, concentrated beam. The basilisk was struck by its own power, turning to stone in an instant. This legend served as a timeless lesson for the people of Rome: that the most absolute power is often its own greatest vulnerability, and that the only way to defeat a monster is to force it to look upon its own true nature.
moral: Even the deadliest power has a weakness; often, the only thing that can defeat a monster is the reflection of its own evil.

title: Sleeping Beauty
summary: The Erl-King is the darkest fairy tale that the Brothers Grimm collected, a poem of terror that has haunted German children for centuries.
full_text:
The Erl-King is the darkest fairy tale that the Brothers Grimm collected, a poem of terror that has haunted German children for centuries. It is the story of a father riding through the forest at midnight, his sick son clutched to his chest.

The father rides with desperate speed. His son shivers with fever, his skin burning against his father's chest. The night is dark, the wind howls through the bare branches. And then the boy hears a whisper.

"Sweet child, come with me," the voice says. "I will play with you. I have flowers of gold and my mother has robes of silk."

The father does not hear the voice. He rides faster, his horse's hooves thundering on the frozen ground. But the boy sees the Erl-King now — a tall, thin figure with a crown of twisted twigs and eyes like dying coals. The Erl-King reaches out with pale fingers that look like birch bark.

"I have daughters who dance in the moonlight," the Erl-King says. "They will dance with you and sing you to sleep."

The father tries to comfort his son. "It is only the wind in the leaves," he says. "It is only the gray mist rising from the river."

But the Erl-King grows impatient. His voice turns from sweet to harsh. "If you will not come willingly, I will take you by force!"

The boy screams. The father digs his heels into his horse and rides with all his might. He reaches the courtyard of his home, gasping for breath. But when he looks down at his son, the boy is dead in his arms.

Goethe's poem "Der Erlkönig" made this story famous, and Schubert's music gave it wings. The Erl-King represents death itself — the final journey that every parent fears, the enemy that no love can outrun. The story is a reminder that some evils cannot be outridden, and that the most terrifying monsters are the ones only children can see.

title: The Enchanted Deer
summary: In the sun-drenched hills of Italy, there once lived a poor hunter who spent his days tracking game through the dense forests that covered the mountainsides.
full_text:
In the sun-drenched hills of Italy, there once lived a poor hunter who spent his days tracking game through the dense forests that covered the mountainsides. He was a solitary man, content with his simple life, but lately he had grown troubled. For weeks, his traps had come up empty, and his family was growing hungry. One morning, as he trudged through the forest with his bow slung over his shoulder, he came upon a deer unlike any he had ever seen. Its coat was pure white, and its antlers seemed to glow with a soft, golden light.

The hunter raised his bow and took aim, but before he could release his arrow, the deer spoke. "Do not kill me," it said in a voice like wind chimes. "I am no ordinary deer. I am under an enchantment, and if you spare my life, I will show you wonders beyond your imagination."

The hunter, though startled, lowered his bow. The deer turned and began to walk deeper into the forest, and the hunter followed. They walked for hours, through thickets and across streams, until they reached a hidden valley the hunter had never seen. In the side of a mountain was a great stone door. The deer touched it with its nose, and the door swung open, revealing a passage that led deep underground.

Inside, the hunter found a kingdom of breathtaking beauty. Crystals grew from the walls like trees, casting rainbows of light across every surface. Rivers of silver flowed through meadows of flowers that glowed with their own inner light. And in the center of this underground realm stood a castle made of pearl and moonstone.

The deer led the hunter through the castle to a great hall where a beautiful princess sat upon a throne. She thanked the hunter for sparing the deer, explaining that she herself was the deer, transformed by a wicked sorcerer's curse. Only by bringing a kind-hearted human into the hidden kingdom could she break the spell. The curse was now broken, and she asked the hunter to stay.

He remained in the underground kingdom, marrying the princess and living in joy. She gave him one warning: "You may explore every room in the castle except the seventh door at the end of the eastern corridor. That door must never be opened."

For months, the hunter was content. But curiosity gnawed at him. One night, while the princess slept, he crept to the seventh door and turned the key. Beyond it, he saw his old village, his elderly mother weeping, his siblings struggling without him. He saw the passage of time in the world above, and he understood that he had been gone for years, not months.

He stepped through the door and found himself in his village, suddenly an old man, his youth spent. The hidden kingdom vanished from his memory like a dream. He spent his remaining years telling the story of the enchanted deer to anyone who would listen, warning them that some doors should never be opened.

The hunter's story passed into legend, and the people of the Italian hills whispered that somewhere, deep beneath the mountains, a hidden kingdom still existed where time flowed differently and enchanted deer roamed through crystalline forests. Some said that on midsummer nights, when the moon was full and the air was still, one could see a faint glow emanating from a crack in the mountainside, the last remnant of the enchanted realm. Children were warned never to enter caves alone, for the kingdom might swallow them and keep them forever. And every hunter who ventured into the deep forests carried the hope, and the fear, that he might one day encounter a deer with antlers that glowed like gold.

title: The Nun's Priest Tale
summary: In the bustling streets of 14th-century Florence, where merchants hawked their wares and the scent of fresh bread mingled with the aroma of roasting meat, there lived a priest whose appetite for earthly pleasures far exceeded his devotion to spiritual matters.
full_text:
In the bustling streets of 14th-century Florence, where merchants hawked their wares and the scent of fresh bread mingled with the aroma of roasting meat, there lived a priest whose appetite for earthly pleasures far exceeded his devotion to spiritual matters. This tale, drawn from Giovanni Boccaccio's masterpiece The Decameron, concerns the comical misadventures of a priest, a stolen chicken, and a peasant girl whose cleverness proved far sharper than any clerical cunning.

The story takes place in the village of Varlungo, not far from Florence, where a priest named Ser—the title given to notaries and priests—served his parish with considerably more attention to the dinner table than the altar. Ser was a robust man with red cheeks and a round belly, evidence of his fondness for good food and fine wine. His particular weakness was for the cooking of Monna Belcolore, a beautiful young married woman whose culinary skills were the talk of the village. Her chickens were plump, her pasta was perfect, and her sauces were legendary.

One afternoon, Ser found himself walking past Monna Belcolore's cottage at precisely the moment she was pulling a golden-brown roasted chicken from her oven. The aroma drifted across the road and seized the priest's senses with irresistible force. He knocked on her door, his mouth already watering. Monna Belcolore, who knew the priest's reputation well, greeted him with a knowing smile. After exchanging pleasantries, Ser made his intentions clear—he wanted that chicken, and he was willing to use both his clerical authority and his cunning to get it.

He offered to hear her confession right then and there, but Monna Belcolore was too clever for such simple tricks. She knew that priests who came calling with empty stomachs usually had more than absolution on their minds. She told him plainly that if he wanted her chicken, he would have to pay for it—not with prayers but with something of real value. Ser, whose purse was as empty as his stomach was full of desire, looked around for something to offer. He spotted his fine woolen cloak, the one he wore for Sunday mass, and reluctantly offered it as a pledge.

Monna Belcolore accepted the cloak and handed over the chicken. Ser ate with great satisfaction, licking his fingers clean. But the next day, his conscience began to prick him—not for the sin of gluttony, but for the loss of his best cloak. He sent his servant to Monna Belcolore's house with a borrowed mortar, asking if she would lend him the one she owned. She sent it back willingly. A few days later, Ser returned the mortar himself, but casually mentioned that he had come to reclaim his cloak as well.

Monna Belcolore was not fooled. "What cloak?" she asked innocently. "You left me no cloak. But since you have returned my mortar, I suppose I should give you back what you left in pledge." She disappeared into her house and emerged a moment later—not with the cloak, but with a handful of chicken bones wrapped in a cloth. "Here," she said with a mischievous smile, "the remains of your dinner. I hope they will keep you as warm as my chicken kept me fed." She laughed heartily, and Ser, realizing he had been outwitted by a peasant woman, could only laugh along. He left with his tail between his legs, having learned that a clever woman is a match for any priest.

Boccaccio's tale is more than a simple comedy. It is a sharp satire of the clergy, who in 14th-century Italy were often more interested in worldly pleasures than spiritual duties. The story pokes fun at the hypocrisy of priests who preached poverty while scheming for good food, and it celebrates the quick wit of ordinary people who see through clerical pretensions. The chicken was more than a chicken—it was a symbol of the earthly appetites that even the holy could not resist, and the laughter it inspired was a reminder that sometimes, the cleverest people are the ones society overlooks.

And so the tale of the priest's stolen chicken was passed down through generations, a reminder that wit and wisdom are not the exclusive province of the educated and powerful, but can be found in the most humble cottages, wherever a chicken is roasting and a clever woman is ready with a well-timed jest.

title: Coviella
summary: In the sun-drenched hills of the Italian countryside, where olive groves stretch across terraced slopes and the scent of rosemary and thyme drifts on the warm breeze, there once lived a young woman named Coviella.
full_text:
In the sun-drenched hills of the Italian countryside, where olive groves stretch across terraced slopes and the scent of rosemary and thyme drifts on the warm breeze, there once lived a young woman named Coviella. Her name, a diminutive of the Italian word for covetousness, meant "little greedy one," and she earned it through her insatiable desire for more. Coviella was not evil, but she was consumed by an appetite for possessions that could never be satisfied. She coveted her neighbor's fine wool shawl, her friend's silver brooch, the merchant's baskets of ripe figs, and the baker's crusty loaves of bread fresh from the stone oven. Her eyes were always on what others had, never grateful for what she possessed.

One day, as Coviella walked through the forest at the edge of her village, she came upon an old woman struggling to carry a bundle of firewood. The woman was bent and weary, her clothes patched and threadbare. Coviella, whose arms were empty, walked right past without offering help. The old woman called out, "Please, young lady, could you spare a moment to help an old woman?" But Coviella, thinking only of the market she was headed to, replied, "I have no time for beggars. The merchant is selling silk ribbons today, and I must have one before they are all gone."

As she spoke, the air around her grew still and cold. The old woman straightened up, and her eyes flashed with an otherworldly light. She was no ordinary old woman but a strega, a powerful witch who tested the hearts of those she encountered. "You who refuse to give even a moment of your time," the strega declared, "shall learn what it means to hold on to everything you desire. From this moment, Coviella, everything you touch shall stick to you until you learn the value of generosity."

At first, Coviella did not believe the curse had taken hold. But as she continued down the path, she reached out to pick a wildflower—and it stuck to her palm. Annoyed, she brushed it against her skirt, but both the flower and the fabric clung to her hand. She tried to shake them off, but they would not release her. Panicking, she touched a tree branch to steady herself, and the branch, the leaves, the bark all adhered to her body. She stumbled into the village, covered in debris, and people stared in astonishment. A child dropped a piece of bread, and when Coviella bent to pick it up, the bread stuck to her hand as well. Soon she was covered in flowers, leaves, twigs, bread, bits of cloth, and anything else she touched.

The village watched as Coviella grew heavier and heavier with the weight of everything she clutched. She could no longer move freely. She could no longer enter her home because the doorframe was too narrow for her burden. She sat by the roadside, weeping, as the objects she had accumulated pressed down upon her. The more she tried to free herself, the more things became attached.

Days passed, and Coviella's suffering grew. Children laughed at her, and adults shook their heads. But then a poor family passed by—a mother with three hungry children, their clothes in tatters. The youngest child, a little girl with big brown eyes, approached Coviella and said, "Signorina, you look so sad. Can I help you?" Touched by the child's compassion, Coviella forgot her own misery for a moment. She looked at the bread stuck to her hand and said, "Little one, I cannot free this bread, but perhaps you can take a bite of it."

The child nibbled the bread, and to Coviella's astonishment, the bread released its hold on her hand. In that moment, she understood. She gave the bread to the hungry family, and it fell away from her completely. She reached out with her other hand and gave away a ribbon, and it too released her. One by one, as she offered her accumulated treasures to those in need, each object fell away. By the time the sun set, Coviella was free—and her heart was transformed.

From that day forward, Coviella became the most generous woman in the village. She gave freely to anyone in need, and the more she gave, the lighter she felt. The curse was broken forever, but its lesson remained: greed binds you, while generosity sets you free.
